petitioner_background: The petitioner is a self-represented actor seeking classification as an employment-based immigrant under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the INA as an alien of extraordinary ability in the arts. Based on the record, he appears to be of Polish origin who had acted in movies in Europe (specifically Poland) between 1981 and 1991 before arriving in the United States in 1991. At the time of filing, an article indicated he was driving an ice cream truck for a living while hoping to reclaim his prior star status. He submitted seven DVD recordings of movies in which he acted as part of his appeal.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: In his April 18, 2005 letter, the petitioner stated that a few of the movies in which he acted won awards. He provided no further details regarding the titles, dates, or significance of these purported awards.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that simply going on record without supporting documentary evidence is not sufficient to meet the burden of proof, citing Matter of Soffici, 22 I&N Dec. 158, 165 (Comm. 1998) (citing Matter of Treasure Craft of California, 14 I&N Dec. 190 (Reg. Comm. 1972)). The record contained no corroborative evidence of any nationally or internationally recognized prizes or awards presented to the petitioner as an individual actor.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted a copy of a letter dated November 26, 1996 from the Screen Actors Guild addressed to him as a member. The letter referenced an enclosed check for 'late payment damages in conjunction with [the petitioner's] work on FULL COURT PRESS.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that the record contained no evidence that the petitioner remained a member of the Screen Actors Guild in the over seven years from the date of this letter to the filing of his petition. The record was also devoid of any evidence that the Screen Actors Guild requires outstanding achievements of its members, as judged by recognized national or international acting experts. Rather, the submitted letter stated that the Screen Actors Guild is a branch of the 'Associated Actors and Artistes of America/AFL-CIO.' The AAO reasoned that insomuch as membership in a labor union is a necessary or regular part of employment in a given field, an alien's union membership alone will not satisfy this category.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted several articles purportedly about him written in foreign languages, without English translations. He also submitted one article in English entitled 'Keeping it All in the Family,' published in the June 22, 1998 edition of an unspecified publication (redacted in the decision). The English article discussed the assimilation of the petitioner's immigrant family into the United States and stated that the petitioner was a successful young movie actor in Poland who now drives an ice cream truck for a living and 'struggles between enjoying his newfound anonymity and hoping to one day reclaim the star status he knew.' On appeal, the petitioner indicated he would send translations of the foreign-language articles within 30 days but never did.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that because the petitioner failed to submit certified translations of the foreign-language documents as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3), it could not determine whether the evidence supported the petitioner's claim, and the evidence was not probative and would not be accorded any weight. Over eight months after the petitioner's June 14, 2005 appeal, the AAO had received nothing further. As to the English-language article, the AAO noted it was published six years before the petition was filed and indicated that even prior to its publication, the petitioner's acclaim as an actor in Europe was in the past. The record was also devoid of any evidence that the publication was a professional, major trade publication, a nationally circulated (as opposed to regional) newspaper, or otherwise constituted a form of major media.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner did not specify any original artistic contributions of major significance he had made to his field. He submitted a letter dated April 15, 2005 from an individual identified as 'Director/Producer' of A&E's Biography 'Fyodor Dostoevsky,' who stated that the petitioner is an extraordinary Actor/Artist, has displayed much professional talent in his assigned roles, and 'could be the future Dustin Hoffman.' On appeal, the petitioner also submitted DVD copies of seven movies in which he acted, dating from 1981 to 1991.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that the letter writer did not state that the petitioner acted in 'Fyodor Dostoevsky' or otherwise discuss how he was aware of the petitioner's abilities. While the letter indicated the petitioner was a talented actor, it did not state that he had made any original artistic contributions of major significance consistent with sustained acclaim. The DVDs submitted lacked certified translations as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3) and thus were not probative. The petitioner submitted no documentation corroborating his claim that the movies aired all over Europe or won awards. Most importantly, the petitioner submitted no evidence that his work in these films was critically acclaimed, influenced other actors, or otherwise had a significant impact on his field. The AAO further noted that the movies dated from 23 to 13 years before the petition was filed, and the record contained no primary evidence of the petitioner's work as an actor after 1991, failing to show sustained acclaim after his arrival in the United States in 1991.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner claimed that he acted in films that were broadcast all over Europe and that some of these films won unspecified awards.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that the record contained no evidence to corroborate these claims or document that the petitioner's work was shown at film festivals or other comparable events in a manner consistent with sustained national or international acclaim. Simply going on record without supporting documentary evidence is not sufficient to meet the burden of proof, citing Soffici, 22 I&N Dec. at 165.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner claimed that he starred in seven movies from 1981 to 1991 that were aired all over Europe and some of which won unspecified awards. He submitted DVD copies of these movies on appeal.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that the copies of the petitioner's movies were submitted without the required translations and the record was devoid of any corroborative evidence of the purportedly wide broadcast of the movies or their alleged awards. Even if the record sufficiently documented the petitioner's starring role in the movies, the petitioner had submitted no evidence that he performed a leading or critical role for any distinguished acting or film company as a whole in a manner consistent with the requisite sustained acclaim.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal, finding that the record did not establish that the petitioner had achieved sustained national or international acclaim as an actor placing him at the very top of his field. An immigrant visa under section 203(b)(1)(A) will only be granted if the alien can establish extraordinary ability through extensive documentation of sustained national or international acclaim demonstrating rising to the very top of the field. The petitioner failed to meet any of the regulatory criteria, failed to submit required certified translations, failed to provide corroborative documentation for his claims, and the record contained no primary evidence of his work as an actor after 1991, failing to demonstrate sustained acclaim after his arrival in the United States. The burden of proof remained entirely with the petitioner under Section 291 of the Act, and he did not sustain that burden.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act
Matter of Treasure Craft of California, 14 I&N Dec. 190 (Reg. Comm. 1972)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Procedural issues: The petitioner failed to submit certified English translations of foreign-language articles and DVD movie recordings as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3), rendering that evidence non-probative. On appeal dated June 14, 2005, the petitioner indicated he would send translations of the articles to the AAO within 30 days, but over eight months later, the AAO had received nothing further from the petitioner. The petitioner was self-represented throughout the proceedings.
